title_en: The Umayyad Caliphate and Its Legacy
content_en:
The Umayyad Caliphate (41–132 AH / 661–750 CE), founded by Mu'awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan and lasting for approximately ninety years, represents one of the most consequential and contested periods in Islamic history. Ibn Kathir's account in al-Bidayah wan-Nihayah is careful to distinguish between the extraordinary civilization-building achievements of the Umayyad era and the theological and moral criticisms that Islamic scholarship has consistently leveled at aspects of Umayyad governance.

Mu'awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan (r. 41–60 AH / 661–680 CE), the first Umayyad Caliph, was a Companion of the Prophet ﷺ who had served as his secretary of revelation. As the governor of Syria for over twenty years before becoming Caliph, he had developed extraordinary administrative and political skills. His agreement with Hasan ibn Ali — in which Hasan peacefully ceded the caliphate — reunited the Muslim community after the divisions of Ali's caliphate, an event the Prophet ﷺ had reportedly foretold and praised: 'This son of mine is a leader (sayyid), and through him, Allah will reconcile two great groups of Muslims.' (Al-Bukhari.)

Under the Umayyads, Islamic civilization achieved extraordinary expansion. The Islamic empire reached its greatest territorial extent, stretching from al-Andalus (Spain and Portugal) in the west through North Africa, the Middle East, Persia, and Central Asia to the borders of the Indian subcontinent in the east — the largest contiguous empire the world had seen since Alexander's brief conquests. The Umayyad Caliph al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik (r. 86–96 AH) oversaw the construction of some of the most magnificent architectural achievements of the early Islamic world: the Umayyad Mosque of Damascus, the expansion and beautification of the Masjid al-Nabawi in Medina, and the Mosque of the Companions at the site of Islam's first mosque in Jerusalem. It was during his reign that Tariq ibn Ziyad crossed the Strait of Gibraltar in 92 AH (711 CE) and rapidly conquered most of the Iberian Peninsula.

The most painful event of the Umayyad period, which Ibn Kathir treats with appropriate solemnity, is the tragedy of Karbala — the killing of Husayn ibn Ali (the Prophet's grandson) and most of his family and companions by the forces of Yazid ibn Mu'awiyah in 61 AH (680 CE). Ibn Kathir's account is historically meticulous and theologically careful: he condemns the killing of Husayn as a grave injustice and a tragedy for the Muslim community, while rejecting the doctrinal conclusions that Shi'a Islam has drawn from it. Husayn's death is mourned as the loss of a righteous man of the Prophet's ﷺ household, not as a cosmic redemptive event.

The Umayyad caliphate was eventually overthrown in 132 AH by the Abbasid revolution — a movement that drew on widespread discontent with Umayyad social policies (particularly their treatment of non-Arab Muslims as second-class) and religious legitimacy concerns. The last Umayyad Caliph, Marwan II, was defeated and killed, and virtually the entire Umayyad ruling family was massacred — except for Abd al-Rahman ibn Mu'awiyah, who escaped to al-Andalus and established an independent Umayyad emirate there that would eventually become the Caliphate of Cordoba.
